skill.name: dogfood
skill.description: Systematically dogfood a web application with agent-browser, capture screenshots and repro videos, and produce a structured issue report for product and engineering teams.
skill.summary: Exploratory QA workflow for finding web app issues with reproducible evidence.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the weak-crypto, blocker, reconnaissance, and upload alerts are Markdown keyword collisions or QA guidance, not malicious code. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill invokes agent-browser against user-provided URLs and writes screenshots, videos, reports, and authentication state that may contain sensitive data. No prompt injection attempt, hidden exfiltration endpoint, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation Against User-Provided URLs
medium_findings.description: The skill is authorized to run Bash agent-browser commands and opens a target URL supplied by the user. This is the core purpose of the skill, but it creates network and browser-automation risk if used against sensitive, internal, or untrusted applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and workflow commands clearly permit agent-browser execution against a user-selected URL. The behavior is legitimate QA automation, so the concern is controlled operational risk rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Test Evidence Stored Locally
medium_findings.description: The workflow records screenshots, repro videos, reports, and saved authentication state under the output directory. These artifacts may contain credentials, session tokens, personal data, or confidential application content if users test private systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly fill login fields, save auth state, and capture screenshots and videos. The data exposure risk depends on the target app, but the artifact creation is confirmed by the skill text.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts point to Markdown prose in the skill description, issue taxonomy, and report template. No cryptographic API, hashing function, encryption routine, or credential obfuscation logic is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation and QA severity text, not executable cryptographic code. This makes the static weak-crypto findings very likely keyword-based false positives.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Upload Alerts Are QA Documentation
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance and screen-capture-upload alerts map to browser testing guidance, console checking, screenshots, and local repro evidence. The skill does not instruct uploading captured files to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands inspect the browser session and create local evidence for QA. No file upload command, external storage endpoint, or host reconnaissance instruction was found.

low_findings.title: Temporary Output Directory Is User-Controlled Local Storage
low_findings.description: The filesystem alert comes from an example output directory and commands that create local report folders. This is necessary for evidence collection, but users should choose a secure location when testing private applications.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicit and limited to user-selected output artifacts. It is not evidence of stealthy persistence or destructive file activity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tooling Executes Browser Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on Bash access for agent-browser commands. The command family is scoped, but it still performs real browser actions, network requests, screenshots, video recording, and local file writes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools and command examples provide direct evidence. The pattern is intentional for this QA skill, but it deserves a marketplace warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: Saved Authentication State
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs saving authenticated browser state to an auth-state file. This can preserve session material locally and should be treated as sensitive output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file path and state-save command are explicit. The exact sensitivity depends on the app, but saved browser state commonly contains authentication material.

content.user_title: Test Web Apps With Repro Evidence
content.value_statement: Manual web QA often misses context, evidence, and clear reproduction steps. This skill guides Codex, Claude, or Claude Code through exploratory testing and produces actionable bug reports.
actual_capabilities: Starts an agent-browser session for a target web application URL.
Captures annotated screenshots and interactive snapshots during exploration.
Checks navigation, forms, states, console errors, responsiveness, and auth boundaries.
Records repro videos for interactive or timing-sensitive issues.
Creates a structured report with severity, category, evidence, and repro steps.
limitations: Requires the agent-browser command to be available in the execution environment.
Does not audit source code, backend logic, or infrastructure configuration.
Authenticated testing requires user-provided credentials or one-time codes.
Screenshots, videos, and auth state may contain sensitive information.

use_cases.title: Pre-Release Product QA
use_cases.description: Run an exploratory pass before launch and collect reproducible issues for the engineering team.

use_cases.title: Regression Bug Hunt
use_cases.description: Exercise core flows after a change and document visual, functional, console, and accessibility problems.

use_cases.title: Founder App Review
use_cases.description: Test a live product quickly and receive a clear report of issues that block user confidence.

prompt_templates.title: Dogfood A Public Site
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood https://example.com with the default workflow. Focus on obvious bugs, UX issues, console errors, and broken navigation.

prompt_templates.title: Test A Specific Area
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood https://example.com and focus on the billing and checkout flows. Capture screenshots for every issue and summarize severity.

prompt_templates.title: Review An Authenticated Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood https://example.com/dashboard. Sign in with the credentials I provide, test account settings, and ask me for any one-time code.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Release Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Dogfood the staging app at https://staging.example.com. Prioritize critical workflows, responsive layouts, console failures, and issues with full repro videos.

output_examples.input: Dogfood a marketing site before launch.
output_examples.output: A concise report listing broken links, clipped mobile text, missing loading feedback, and screenshots showing each problem.
A reproducible issue report with login context, step-by-step screenshots, a repro video for the broken flow, and severity counts.

output_examples.input: Check a release candidate for regressions.
output_examples.output: A prioritized QA summary covering core flows, console errors, visual defects, and the most important fixes to address before release.

best_practices: Use a test account and avoid production secrets when testing authenticated applications.
Set a dedicated output directory and treat captured artifacts as sensitive data.
Give a focused scope when the app is large or the release risk is concentrated.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill as a replacement for source-code security review.
Do not test third-party applications without authorization.
Do not store screenshots, videos, or auth state in public repositories.

faq.question: What does this skill test?
faq.answer: It tests a web application through the browser and looks for visual, functional, UX, console, performance, and accessibility issues.

faq.question: Does it need access to source code?
faq.answer: No. The workflow explicitly tests as a user and relies on browser observations, screenshots, console output, and repro steps.

faq.question: Can it test logged-in pages?
faq.answer: Yes. You must provide credentials and any one-time codes. Use a test account whenever possible.

faq.question: What files does it create?
faq.answer: It creates a report, screenshots, repro videos, and optionally a saved browser authentication state file.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential applications?
faq.answer: Use caution. Captured artifacts can include private data, so store outputs securely and delete them when no longer needed.

faq.question: Does it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It may notice user-visible security issues, but it is designed for product QA, not penetration testing or code security audit.
